classification_notes: The foundry explicitly cites didones as the inspiration, with high contrast between thick and thin strokes, delicate hairlines and an 18th-century French aesthetic consistent with the Bodoni/Didot lineage. Marketed and structured as a display-only typeface.
summary: A high-contrast didone display serif drawing on 18th-century French typographic tradition and the aesthetic of Romantic-era handwriting.
description: Designed with overt reference to the didone tradition of Bodoni and Didot, the typeface pairs dramatic stroke contrast with delicate hairlines suited to large-scale display settings. The foundry frames it around the culture of late 18th-century France, citing Napoleon, the Romantic movement and the craft of traditional French bookbinding as points of reference. With over 590 glyphs and broad Latin language coverage, it targets editorial, identity and luxury brand contexts.
foundry_blurb: Almarena describe 1769 Display as inspired by the handwritten curves of the Romantic movement and the birth of Napoleon Bonaparte in that year. They draw a direct line to the didone tradition, explaining how those typefaces dominated 19th-century French printing before falling out of favour with the arrival of low-resolution screens. The foundry positions the typeface as a modern revival of that style, citing Bodoni's continued use in fashion publishing and framing 1769 Display as a symbol of French elegance and cultural heritage.

name: Almarena
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly situates Almarena within the lineal/grotesk tradition rooted in 19th-century grotesques, describing it as a contemporary continuation of that lineage with optical refinements and no ornament.
summary: A French lineal grotesque in two optical styles — Classic and Display — designed for brand identities and editorial work where restraint is preferred over flourish.
description: Released in 2020 by Jérémie Gauthier, Almarena offers three weights across two distinct styles: Classic, tuned for text and running copy, and Display, built for larger settings and headlines. The drawing favours optical balance over strict geometry, with carefully adjusted terminals and curves that keep the typeface legible without feeling mechanical. Its Latin Extended coverage spans a broad range of European languages, making it serviceable for multilingual brand work.
foundry_blurb: Almarena describes its typeface as a lineal drawn with precision and designed to last, built on three qualities: clarity, discipline and character. The foundry situates it within the grotesk tradition of the 19th century, noting its debt to early lineals designed for posters and signage, while positioning the contemporary drawing as a calm, structured and optically refined continuation of that lineage.

name: Almarena Monospaced
classification_notes: A fixed-width typeface with geometric construction, available in both classic-spaced and true fixed-width variants, placing it squarely in the monospaced category with geometric sans characteristics.
summary: A geometric monospaced typeface in two styles and two weights, available in classic-spaced or true fixed-width cuts, designed with a mechanical character drawn from typewriter and coding traditions.
description: Designed by Jérémie Gauthier and released in 2022, the family offers Classic and Display styles, each in Light and Bold, with the option of either proportional-feeling classic spacing or genuine fixed-width output under the FW label. The geometric construction, with its calculated angles and deliberate curves, gives it an affinity with arcade typography, festival posters and technical interfaces. Multiple stylistic sets provide alternate letterforms for applications where tighter character is needed.
foundry_blurb: Almarena describe Monospaced as mechanically geometric with a robotic, technological air, citing influences ranging from typewriter typography and source-code readability to arcade video games and the modularity of LEGO bricks. They position it as a companion to the original Almarena Typeface, suited to concert posters, web use and urban design contexts, and note its availability in both classic and true fixed-width spacing variants.

name: Almarena Neue
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly classifies it as a lineal/grotesk in the tradition of 19th-century grotesques, with ink traps and a sans-serif construction free of serifs and ornament.
summary: A variable grotesque by Jérémie Gauthier offered in Classic and Display optical styles across nine weights with italics, drawing on the 19th-century lineal tradition.
description: Released in 2023, it operates on three variable axes covering weight, display contrast and italic, giving designers a single file that spans from text to large-format use. The Classic style carries ink traps and thin hairlines intended to give it more character than a straight Helvetica derivative, while the Display style opens up contrast for headline work. Language support runs across a broad range of Latin-based languages from Afrikaans to Zulu.
foundry_blurb: Almarena describe Almarena Neue as a model from the past reimagined for today, combining modernity, readability and originality. They draw a direct line to the first lineals of the 19th century, positioning the typeface within a grotesk tradition while pointing to its ink traps and hairlines as the details that set it apart from Helvetica. They cite Apple and Chanel as brands already aligned with this style of lineal.

name: Goïa
classification_notes: Goïa is a geometric sans with rounded terminals and a constructivist influence, referencing the Bauer and ITC aesthetic canons. Its circular forms and rationalist structure place it firmly in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A geometric variable sans by Jérémie Gauthier, offered in Classic and Display optical styles across nine weights with two variation axes.
description: Designed by Jérémie Gauthier and released in 2023, Goïa draws on the rationalist tradition of Bauer and ITC foundries whilst introducing softened, rounded forms. The family ships as a variable font with weight and italic axes, alongside static OTF files for nine weights per style. Classic and Display subfamilies allow it to move between running text and headline work without compromise.
foundry_blurb: Almarena describe Goïa as a modern, balanced geometric sans rooted in constructivism and inspired by the aesthetic standards of the Bauer and ITC foundries. They position it as versatile enough to handle logos, headlines and body copy, with its variable font axes offering flexibility across weight and tilt. The foundry emphasise its rounded, precise forms and a character that sits between classical rigour and a hint of whimsy.

name: Joyse
classification_notes: A display sans-serif with exaggerated curves and sharp angles drawn in four distinct stylistic variants; its vintage-inflected, high-contrast character places it firmly in the display category rather than a neutral sans-serif.
summary: A display sans by Jérémie Gauthier with four stylistic variants, blending exaggerated curves with sharp breaks drawn from post-modernist and vintage graphic sources.
description: Released in 2025, Joyse spans nine weights per style, each with matching italics, across four subfamilies: Original, Straight, Smooth and Classic. The design draws on the visual language of vinyl sleeve lettering and neon signage, pairing generous rounded forms with abrupt angular cuts. Available as both static OTF files and a variable font with weight and italic axes, it is aimed at logotypes, poster work and editorial contexts where strong graphic presence is needed.
foundry_blurb: Almarena describe Joyse as a display typeface that embraces the sensual decadence of vintage, sitting at the crossroads of roundness and sharp breaks. They frame it as an ode to post-modernist design excess, with sculpted glyphs evoking vinyl sleeves, neon lights and the golden age of plastic. The foundry positions it for logotypes, posters and magazine covers where a bold typographic statement is required.

name: Kassi
classification_notes: Kassi is a heavily condensed, high-contrast display typeface with angular letterforms and references to gothic fraktur and impact-style lineals, placing it firmly in the display category with a condensed display subcategory.
summary: A two-style ultra-condensed display face by Jérémie Gauthier, blending angular geometry with echoes of gothic fraktur and impact-style lineals.
description: Released in 2022, Kassi comes in Classic and Display variants, both sharing a high x-height, compressed counters and strongly marked verticals that nod towards German typographic tradition and early lineal grotesques like Impact. The angular letterforms and tight interletter spacing make it best suited to headlines, large-format print and display use where presence matters. Its broad Latin language coverage extends to over eighty languages across Western and Eastern European scripts.
foundry_blurb: Almarena describe Kassi as a modernised lineal descended from the first lineals, tightening up in the tradition of Geoffrey Lee's Impact with compressed counterpunches, a large x-height and very tight interletter spacing. They note its angular forms and broken curves give it an air of gothic fraktur, and that it pays homage to astronomer Jacques Cassini through its precision and contemporaneity. Designed by Jérémie Gauthier with art direction by Gauthier and Romain Billaud, it is positioned for headlines, web use, animation and large-format print.

name: Kogi
classification_notes: Kogi is a serif typeface distinguished by rounded terminals and softened serifs throughout, placing it firmly in the rounded serif subcategory rather than a classical or transitional serif tradition.
summary: A rounded serif with generous curves and softened terminals, designed for warm, expressive work in food, packaging and branding contexts.
description: Built around very generous curves and rounded serifs, Kogi aims to balance warmth with structural clarity across nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic. The family ships as both variable and static fonts with two axes for weight and italic, and includes over 600 glyphs covering a broad range of Latin-script languages. Six stylistic sets and a selection of discretionary ligatures extend its expressive range for display and identity work.
foundry_blurb: Almarena describe Kogi as a typeface where soft power meets bold presence, defined by generous curves and rounded serifs that deliver impact without stiffness. Designed by Jérémie Gauthier with art direction by Gauthier and Romain Billaud, it is positioned as particularly suited to food-related projects where appetite appeal and warmth matter, while remaining versatile enough for logos, posters, packaging and visual identities.

name: VHS Fitness Family
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly classifies this as a display sans-serif; the single-weight, VHS-referencing aesthetic confirms its purpose as a headline and display face rather than a text typeface.
summary: A free display sans-serif with a single weight, drawing on VHS-era aesthetics for headline and campaign use.
description: Designed by Jérémie Gauthier with art direction shared between Gauthier and Romain Billaud, this display sans references the visual language of VHS-era graphics. It ships in one weight with an italic, in OTF and WOFF formats, and covers an extensive range of Latin-script languages. Available free to download, with commercial merchandising use requiring separate licensing confirmation.
foundry_blurb: Almarena describe VHS Fitness Family as a display sans-serif typeface released in 2024, available as a free download for most uses. Merchandising projects require contacting the foundry for confirmation and pricing. The family covers one style plus italic and supports a broad set of Latin-based languages.
